coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots encapsulated in super small platinum nanocrystals core-shell architecture/nitrogen doped graphene hybrid nanocomposite for electrochemical biosensing of DNA damage biomarker-8-hydroxy-2′-deoxyguanosine
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Qi
Zhao, Qiuyue
Fu, Mingxuan
Fan, Xinyu
Lu, Haijun
Wang, Haiyang
Zhang, Yufan
Wang, Huan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, carbon quantum dots (CQD) encapsulated in super small platinum nanocrystals core-shell architecture/nitrogen doped graphene hybrid nanocomposite (CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR) was design synthesized. Without using any capping reagent, stabilizer and surfactant, very small CQD was served as template and anchoring point for the synthesis of Pt NCs with a super small size (2.25 nm) and a uniform distribution. Meanwhile, dopamine (DA) was used as bridging agent, positioning agent and weak reducing agent to make Pt2+ grow on the CQD. Combine the high dispersed Pt NCs with high specific surface area and high conductivity of NGR, the CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR shows excellent electrocatalytic performance towards the biosensing of DNA damage biomarker- 8-Hydroxy-2′-deoxyguanosine (8-OH-dG). A very low detection limit of 0.45 nM and 0.85 nM (S/N = 3), a wide linear range of 0.013 μM–109.78 μM and a high sensitivity of 7.912 μA μM−1cm−2 and 4.190 μA μM−1cm−2 were obtained. The fabricated CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR realized the detection of 8-OH-dG in human urine practical sample. Furthermore, CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR was applied for the determination of 8-OH-dG generated from damaged DNA and damaged guanine (G), respectively. This work effectively combines the electrochemical signal of 8-OH-dG with DNA damage, confirms the mechanism of DNA damage, which might pave a new way to establish the associations between degree of DNA damage and 8-OH-dG.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Super small platinum nanocrystals
Nitrogen doped graphene
DNA damage
8-Hydroxy-2′-deoxyguanosine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The repeatability, reproducibility and stability research

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The pictures of CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-(1–5) suspension before and after storage for 7 days.
TEM image of carbon quantum dots (A), inset: TEM image of CQD@PtNCs, TEM images of CQD@PDA@PtNCs-1(B), CQD@PDA@PtNCs-2(C), CQD@PDA@PtNCs-3(D), CQD@PDA@Pt NCs-4 (E) and CQD@PDA@Pt NCs-5 (F).

EDS mapping profiles of CQD@PDA@PtNCs-1, element profiles for (A) C-O-N-Pt overlap, (B) C (blue), (C) O (yellow), (D) N (purple) and (E) Pt (green). (F) EDX spectra of CQD@PDA@Pt NCs-1. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

TEM images of the CQD@PtNCs-NGR (A),inset: the corresponding particle size distribution, CQD@PDA@Pt NCs-NGR-1 (B), CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-2 (C), CQD@PDA@Pt NCs-NGR-3 (D), inset: the corresponding particle size distribution, CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-4 (E) and CQD@PDA@Pt NCs-NGR-5 (F).

(A) XRD patterns of CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-1 (a), CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-2 (b), CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3 (c), CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-4 (d), CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-5 (e). (B) XPS spectra of CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-1 (a), CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-2 (b), CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3 (c), CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-4 (d), CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-5 (e). (C) High resolution Pt XPS spectrum of CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3. (D) High resolution N 1s XPS spectrum of CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3.

(A) TG of CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-1, CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-2, CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3, CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-4, CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-5 in air. (B) CVs of CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-1/GCE, CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-2/GCE, CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE, CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-4/GCE, CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-5 in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, scan rate: 50 mV s−1.

(A) CVs recorded for 32.0 μM 8-OH-dG in 0.1 M pH 7.4 PBS at (a) CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-1/GCE, (b)CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-2/GCE, (c)CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE, (d)CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-4/GCE, (e)CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-5/GCE, (f)NGR/GCE, (g)CQD@PtNCs/GCE, (h) CQD@Pt NCs-NGR/GCE, (i) bare GCE. Scan rate: 50 mV s−1. (B) EIS of CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-1/GCE, CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-2/GCE, CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE, CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-4/GCE, CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-5/GCE, NGR/GCE and bare GCE in the 1:1 solution of 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3-/4- and 0.1 M KCl. The frequency range is from 1.00 Hz to 100 KHz. Inset: randles equivalent circuit.

(A) CVs of 32.0 μM 8-OH-dG on CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE in 0.1 M PBS at different pH: 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10; Scan rate: 50 mV s−1. (B) The corresponding plot for pH vs. Peak potential and peak currents, respectively. (C) chronocoulometry (CC) curves for CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) in the present of 9.78 μM 8-OH-dG.

(A) CVs of 32.0 μM 8-OH-dG at CQD@PDA@Pt NCs-NGR-3/GCE with different scan rates (5–500 mV s−1) in 0.1 M pH 7.0 PBS. (B) The calibration curve of peak current versus scan rate. (C) The calibration curve of E
pa vs Log scan rate.

(A) DPVs of CQD@PDA@Pt NCs-NGR-3/GCE in different concentration of 8-OH-dG to 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0). Inset of (A): The DPVs curves of 8-OH-dG at CQD@PDA@Pt NCs-NGR-3/GCE with low concentrations (a–e: 0, 0.013, 0.093, 0.45, 1.12 μM). (B) Calibration curves between different concentrations of 8-OH-dG and the peak current. Pulse period: 0.0500 s. Pulse amplitude: 0.0250 V. Pulse width: 0.0500 V. DPVs have been processed by baseline correction.

(A) DPVs of CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE in (a) 0.1 M PBS (pH 5.0) (blank); (b) Fenton reagents; (c) undamaged DNA; (d) damaged DNA; (e) damaged DNA+0.391 μg/mL 8-OH-dG. Scan rate: 100 mVs−1. (B) DPVs of CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE in (a) 0.1 M PBS (pH 5.0) (blank); (b) Fenton reagents; (c) undamaged G; (d) damaged G; (e) damaged G + 0.908 μg/mL 8-OH-dG. Scan rate: 100 mVs−1.

(A) CVs for 19.6 μM 8-OH-dG in the presence of 5.2–644.8 μM UA at the CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE in 0.1 M pH 7.0 PBS, scan rate: 50 mV s−1. Inset plots are the anodic peak currents of 8-OH-dG and UA vs. the concentration of UA. (B) CVs for 41.7 μM UA in the presence of 5.3–102.4 μM 8-OH-dG at the CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE in 0.1 M pH 7.0 PBS, scan rate: 50 mV s−1. Inset plots are the anodic peak currents of 8-OH-dG and UA vs. the concentration of 8-OH-dG.
Schematic illustration for the synthesis of carbon dots encapsulated in super small platinum nanocrystals core-shell architecture/nitrogen doped graphene hybrid nanocomposite.
The oxidation mechanism of 8-OH-dG at CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE.
Comparison of the performance of the proposed electrode for the determination of 8-OH-dG with recently reported works.
Results of 8-OH-dG analysis in urine sample.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots encapsulated in super small platinum nanocrystals core-shell architecture/nitrogen doped graphene hybrid nanocomposite for electrochemical biosensing of DNA damage biomarker-8-hydroxy-2′-deoxyguanosine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Analytical Science and Technology of Hebei Province, Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemistry and Molecular Diagnosis, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Environmental Science, Hebei University, Baoding, 071002, Hebei Province, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Analytical Science and Technology of Hebei Province
Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemistry and Molecular Diagnosis
College of Chemistry and Environmental Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, carbon quantum dots (CQD) encapsulated in super small platinum nanocrystals core-shell architecture/nitrogen doped graphene hybrid nanocomposite (CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR) was design synthesized. Without using any capping reagent, stabilizer and surfactant, very small CQD was served as template and anchoring point for the synthesis of Pt NCs with a super small size (2.25 nm) and a uniform distribution. Meanwhile, dopamine (DA) was used as bridging agent, positioning agent and weak reducing agent to make Pt2+ grow on the CQD. Combine the high dispersed Pt NCs with high specific surface area and high conductivity of NGR, the CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR shows excellent electrocatalytic performance towards the biosensing of DNA damage biomarker- 8-Hydroxy-2′-deoxyguanosine (8-OH-dG). A very low detection limit of 0.45 nM and 0.85 nM (S/N = 3), a wide linear range of 0.013 μM–109.78 μM and a high sensitivity of 7.912 μA μM−1cm−2 and 4.190 μA μM−1cm−2 were obtained. The fabricated CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR realized the detection of 8-OH-dG in human urine practical sample. Furthermore, CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR was applied for the determination of 8-OH-dG generated from damaged DNA and damaged guanine (G), respectively. This work effectively combines the electrochemical signal of 8-OH-dG with DNA damage, confirms the mechanism of DNA damage, which might pave a new way to establish the associations between degree of DNA damage and 8-OH-dG.

ce:section.ce:para: Catalysts from platinum nanostructure have been extensively utilized for electrocatalytic reactions owing to their intrinsic catalytic properties [1,2]. However, in the application of Pt nanocatalysts, some issues, such as the high cost, activity and durability should be fully considered. The rational design and synthesis of Pt nanocatalysts with high specific surface area is an effective strategy to cut the cost, improve the catalytic activity and durability. The specific surface area of Pt nanocatalysts is usually determined by its microstructure and morphology. Accordingly, various approaches have been established to synthesize Pt nanostructures with different shapes, such as nanospheres, nanowires, mesoporous structures and so on [3–7]. Besides, small-sized Pt nanocrystals (Pt NCs) usually exhibit a higher specific surface area, thus result in higher electrocatalytic activity [8,9]. Until now, several studies on controllable synthesis of small size Pt NCs to improve catalytic performance have been reported. Among them, core-shell Au@Pt NCs with a mean diameter of 88 nm was prepared using 2-pyrrolidone-5-carboxylic acid sodium salt (PCA-Na) as a growth-directing agent [10]. Li et al. made use of sodium bromide (NaBr) as a capping agent and poly vinylpyrrolidone (PVP) as a stabilizer to synthesize Pt-based ternary alloy PtCuNi nanocrystals with diameter of 7–9 nm [11]. Jiang and co-works prepared Pt NCs with diameter of 6.2 nm via a homogeneous aqueous chemical reduction method using PVP as a stabilizer [12]. Neenu Varghese employed PVP as a capping agent to synthesize Pt NCs with diameter of 4.8 nm [13]. Pt NCs with diameter of 3.0 nm was fabricated via using PVP as a capping agent [14]. These are excellent works of preparing Pt NCs with controllable size, thus, leading to high specific surface area and electrocatalytic activity.

However, in these works, capping agents and guide agents are usually employed, result in time consuming, high–cost, and complicated reaction procedures, which limit manufacture on large–scale Pt nanomaterials. Noticeably, these additives are customarily difficult to remove from nanocrystal surfaces, thereby critically influencing the catalytic efficiency of Pt nanomaterials. Moreover, the size of the prepared Pt NCs is relatively large [15]. Hence, it remains an interesting challenge to develop a capping agent, guide agent and stabilizer free route to direct synthesis of more smaller Pt NCs.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are novel carbon nanostructure that are less than 10 nm in dimension [16]. They have attracted increasing interest owing to their novel, unique and benign nature. The structure and abundant functional groups on the surface of CQD give a diverse application in many industries [17]. Although CQD have many excellent properties, these advantages such as small particle size have not been widely used, especially by utilizing CQD as the location point for preparing small Pt NCs. In this paper, hence, we introduce CQD served as template and anchoring point for the synthesis of Pt NCs with a small size and a uniform distribution. Significantly, the addition of CQD can not only guide Pt NCs grow directionally, but also act as nucleus to control the size of Pt NCs. Meanwhile, in order to further enhance the positioning effect for Pt NCs growth, DA was employed as linking reagent and weak reduction reagent for the assisted synthesis of CQD encapsulated in super small Pt NCs core-shell architecture. Furthermore, to realize efficient utilization of Pt NCs and improve its electrocatalytic properties, nitrogen doped graphene (NGR) with good conductivity, tunable surface functional groups and high surface area was used as a carrier to load Pt NCs [18].

8-Hydroxy-2′-deoxyguanosine (8-OH-dG) has received increasing attention owing to its significance as biomarker of DNA damage [19], cancer and diabetes [20]. Recently, various techniques have been employed to analyze the damaged DNA and 8-OH-dG, respectively, such as HPLC [21], micellar electro kinetic capillary chromatography [22], fluorescence [23] and magnetic solid phase extraction method [24].

Here, in this report, CQD encapsulated in super small Pt NCs core-shell architecture/NGR hybrid nanocomposites are synthesized by a facile and environment-friendly strategy without using any capping reagent and stabilizer. Then, the proposed nanocomposites were applied to measure DNA damage biomarker-8-Hydroxy-2′-deoxyguanosine. This work will be potential to estimate the DNA damage and offer valuable information on diagnosing diseases in immunity system.

8-Hydroxy-2′-deoxyguanosine (8-OH-dG), guanine (G), dopamine (DA), salicylic acid, uric acid (UA), deoxyribonucleic acid (dsDNA) sodium salt from calf thymus, tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (MO, USA). N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), L-ascorbic acid (L-AA), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), hydrochloric acid (HCl), ferrous chloride (FeCl2), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), anhydrous ethanol, potassium chloroplatinate (K2PtCl4), potassium chloride (KCl), potassium ferrocyanide (K4Fe(CN)6·3H2O), potassium ferricyanide (K3Fe(CN)6), phosphoric acid (H3PO4) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.0) was employed as a supporting electrolyte, which consists of NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4 and Na3PO4. Ultrapure water was used throughout all the experiments.

All of the electrochemical experiences were applied in a three electrode cell by using an Autolab Electrochemistry Workstation (PGSTAT 302 N, Metrohm, Switzerland). Working electrode was the modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE, diameter: 3.0 mm). Meanwhile, an Pt wire served as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl served as the reference electrode, respectively. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the fabricated samples were measured with a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) operating at 200 kV. X-Ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were acquired on an X-ray D/max-2200vpc (Rigaku Corporation, Japan) instrument managed at 40 kV and 20 mA using Cu Kα radiation (k = 0.15406 nm). Photoelectron Spectrometer (XPS) was done applying Thermo ESCA LAB spectrometer (USA). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) (TGA Q500) was performed to determine changes in weight in relation to a change in temperature. The temperature was increased by 10 °C per minute to 800 °C under ambient conditions.

A typical synthesis of CQDs was performed as follows [25]: salicylic acid (0.20 g) was dissolved in distilled water (10.0 mL) and stirred for 5 min. The above cloudy solution was put in a Teflon oven vessel and placed in the drying oven at 180 °C for 3 h. The mixture was cooled to room temperature and then centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 20 min in order to remove large particles, the supernatant was passed through a dialysis membrane (MWCO: 500–1000) for eliminating any impurities.

Tris (0.1309 g) was dissolved in 9.0 mL of CQDs solution by stirring until Tris was dissolved. DA (0.006 g) was added into the above solution and stirred for 12 h at room temperature in dark condition. The CQDs@PDA-1 was obtained by centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 5 min and washed with ultrapure water for three times. The above CQDs@PDA-1 was dispersed in the mixture of 6.0 mL ultrapure water and 3.0 mL K2PtCl4 (15.0 mM). In addition, the reaction was kept ultrasonic for 30 min and stirring for 4 h at 90 °C under water bath, the product (CQDs@PDA@Pt NCs-1) was obtained by centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 5 min and washed with ultrapure water for three times.

CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-2 and CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-4 were synthesized through the similar method except that the concentration of 15 mM K2PtCl4 was decreased to 7.5 and 3.75 mM. The synthesis steps for CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-3 and CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-5 were also similar to CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-1, except that the amounts of DA were decreased to 0.003 g and 0.0015 g, meanwhile, the concentration of 15 mM K2PtCl4 was decreased to 7.5 and 3.75 mM.

Graphene oxide (GO) was prepared by using the modified Hummers method. GO (900.0 mg) was dissolved in 900 mL deionized water by sonication until GO was dispersed. Tris (1092.0 mg) was dispersed in the above solution and then sonication for 10 min. Then, dopamine (1800.0 mg) was added to the mixture that was mechanically stirred for 24 h at room temperature, and the obtained mixture were centrifuged and washed with deionized water for three times and then dried at 60 °C for 24 h, then GO/PDA was obtained. Finally, the GO/PDA composite was transformed into NGR after calcination at 800 °C under N2 condition.

NGR (0.005 g) and ultrapure water (8.0 mL) was added into the aforementioned CQDs@PDA@Pt NCs-(1–5), then the solution was ultrasonic for 1 h and stirred for 2 h at room temperature. Subsequently, the obtained mixture was centrifuged, washed with deionized water for three times. After the above steps, product CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-(1–5) was obtained. The preparation procedure of CQDs@PDA@Pt NCs-NGR was listed in Scheme 1 .

Before modifying, the GCE was polished with 1.0, 0.30 and 0.05 μm alumina slurry, then washed by ultrapure water and sonicated for 30 s in anhydrous ethanol and nitric acid, respectively. Then, 1.0 mg of the as-prepared CQDs@PDA@Pt NCs-NGR-(1–5) samples were dispersed into 0.5 mL DMF to form a homogeneous suspension via sonication. Finally, 5.0 μL of the suspension was dropped onto the polished GCE and the solvent was dried under an infrared lamp.

Stability (storage time) of CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR–(1–5) suspension has been investigated. The CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-(1–5) samples were dispersed in DMF to form a homogeneous suspension via sonication for 24 h. CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-(1–5) suspension was uniform dispersion until standing for 7 days. The contrast pictures of the CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-(1–5) before and after storage are shown in Fig. 1 . The result shows that CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-(1–5) suspension has good stability and can store for a longer time.
Actually, the CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-(1–5) suspension was ultrasonic treatment for 30 min before each use, so, the suspension was kept homogeneous for every experiment.

0.05 M L-AA (0.8 mL) and 4.0 mL dsDNA solution (1 mg/mL, solvent: ultrapure water) was added to 0.8 mL FeCl2 solution (0.01 M), the above mixture was continuously stirred for 5 min. Next, 9.79 M H2O2 was diluted 33 times to obtain 0.3 M H2O2 (0.8 mL), and it was putted into the above solution and transferred to 25.0 mL distilling flask. Then, the above solution was heated at 36 °C under oil bath for 40 min. All the above solutions were prepared with ultrapure water. Finally, the damaged DNA was collected and allowed to quickly cool at 4 °C before use. The pH of damaged DNA (0.625 mg/mL) was 2.50. In this process, hydroxyl radicals (·OH) was produced by the reaction of H2O2 and FeCl2 (V Fe 2+:V H2O2 = 1:1) [26], and DNA damage was induced by ·OH [27]. Otherwise, the damaged G was acquired using the same method as the damaged DNA, except that G was dissolved with 4 M NaOH and the pH of damaged G was 11.30.

The structure and morphology of the resulting composites were examined in detail by TEM. It can be clearly seen that numbers of CQD are well dispersed, the average size is 0.22 nm (Fig. 2 A). TEM image of the CQD@PtNCs is shown in inset of Fig. 2A, which illustrates an aggregated and overlapped CQD@PtNCs. This situation is probably due to the random deposit of PtNCs in the absence of DA as a linking agent. Compared with CQD@PtNCs, when DA (0.006 g) is added, the TEM image of CQD@PDA@PtNCs-1 shows more evenly distributed nanostructure, and it can clearly see that there are multiple CQD encapsulated in a PtNCs (Fig. 2B). Additionally, the mean size of CQD@PDA@PtNCs-1 is about 2.53 nm. Fig. 2C–F shows the analogous nanomorphology, the mean size of 2.35, 2.26, 2.30 and 3.13 nm were obtained for CQD@PDA@PtNCs-2 (C), CQD@PDA@PtNCs-3 (D), CQD@PDA@PtNCs-4 (E) and CQD@PDA@PtNCs-5 (F), respectively. The size of the above-mentioned samples is quite different, which due to the different amount of precursors. Clearly, compared with other samples, CQD@PDA@PtNCs-3 illustrates a highly dispersed and remarkably uniform morphology. Moreover, the lattice structure of CQD@PDA@PtNCs-3 is clearly found in the HRTEM image (Fig. 2D), the lattice spacing distance is 0.20 nm, corresponding to the (220) lattice of face centered cubic (fcc) Pt, indicating the fcc Pt is formed in CQD@PDA@Pt NCs-3.

As shown in Fig. 3 B-E, element mapping was further determine the element distribution of C (blue), O (yellow), N (purple) and Pt (green) in CQD@PDA@PtNCs-3. Furthermore, we can clearly see that the overlap of all elements in CQD@PDA@PtNCs-1 (Fig. 3A), suggesting that the composition of the final product is PtNCs encapsulated CQD. The all elements in CQD@PDA@PtNCs-3 are further determined by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) (Fig. 3F), which indicates that all the above elements exist in CQD@PDA@PtNCs-3. All of these indicate the growth of PtNCs on CQD, thus forming a highly dispersed CQD encapsulated in Pt NCs core-shell architecture.

The morphology of the final samples was further studied by TEM. Fig. 4 B–F shows the morphology of CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-(1–5), it displays that each PtNCs encapsulates multiple CQD, which evenly loaded on NGR. While the TEM image of CQD@PtNCs-NGR (without PDA) in Fig. 4A indicates the aggregated PtNCs on the NGR. The statistical analysis based on randomly selecting CQD@PtNCs-NGR from TEM image and measuring their sizes was carried out to evaluate the size distribution, the result shows the particle size ranges are from 2.87 to 3.47 nm, with an average diameter of 3.14 nm (insert of Fig. 4A). The HRTEM image of the CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-2 clearly depicts fringes at the edge, suggesting the existence of NGR (Fig. 4C). The above TEM analysis confirms that Pt-related nanocrystals are well loaded on the NGR. It is obvious that the morphology of CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3 is the best, and the dispersion of PtNCs on NGR is more homogeneous. The particle size ranges are from 2.10 to 2.40 nm, and the average diameter is 2.25 nm, as illustrated by the size histogram insert in Fig. 4D. Thus, the uniformly dispersed CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3 affords larger interface area for electrocatalysis.

In order to determine the crystalline phase of Pt nanoparticles, X-ray power diffraction (XRD) was carried out. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the XRD patterns of five samples exhibit almost the same four diffraction peaks, which are obtained at 2θ = 39.91°, 46.46°, 67.97° and 81.75°, indexed to the {111}, {200}, {220} and {311} diffractions of Pt standard peak, respectively. Hence, the patterns in Fig. 5A confirmed the phase of Pt nanocrystals structure was fcc. Compared with CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-2 (curve b), CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3 (curve c), CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-4 (curve d) and CQD@PDA@Pt NCs-NGR-5 (curve e), the peak shape of CQD@PDA@Pt NCs-NGR-1 (curve a) is more acute and obvious, which is due to the addition of a large amount of Pt precursor.

XPS was used to investigate the chemical bonding and surface composition of all samples (Fig. 5B–D). XPS spectra exhibits three peaks located at 73.5, 284.5 and 531.5 eV correspond to Pt4f, C1s and O1s, respectively (Fig. 5B). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Pt 4f shows two major peaks at 71.3 eV and 74.6 eV that correspond to Pt 4f7/2 and Pt 4f5/2, respectively (Fig. 5C). The Pt 4f spectrum was deconvoluted into four peaks. The peaks with higher intensity (larger peak area) at 71.64 eV and 74.79 eV derived from Pt(0), the peaks with lower intensity (smaller peak area) at 72.69 eV and 75.63 eV derived from Pt(II), respectively [28]. The result shows the main existence of Pt(0) and very low amount existence of Pt(II) in CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3 sample. The above XPS spectra indicate that the PtNCs have been successfully loaded onto the NGR. In addition, the high-resolution N 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 5D) [29] reveals the presence of pyridinic N (N1, 398.0 eV), pyrrolic N (N2, 400.0 eV) and graphitic N (N3, 401.3 eV), indicating that three forms of N were presence in CQD@PDA@Pt NCs-NGR-3.

TG is a very effective technology for analyzing thermal stability and composition of the material, further testing the relationship between the temperature and the quality of the sample under the programmed temperature. As shown in Fig. 6 A, TG was conducted for the as-fabricated samples in air to observe the conversion process during calcination. Principally, weight of all fabricated samples decreases slowly between 30 °C and 330 °C; this is expected to mainly root in the removal of chemically adsorbed H2O and thermal decomposition. Moreover, it can be clearly seen that there is no obvious weight change above 600 °C, indicating that the only existence of Pt above 600 °C. The TG data reveals that the content of Pt in CQDs@PDA@Pt NCs-NGR-(1–5) are 6.12%, 6.80%, 7.88%, 7.95% and 6.77%, respectively.

All fabricated samples were investigated by CVs in 0.5 M H2SO4 for further calculating the electrochemically active surface area (EAS). As shown in Fig. 6B, according to the CV records, the calculation was performed according to the following equation:
A =
Q
H
Q
S
, where A is active surface area of catalyst (in cm2), Q H is charge transferred from the stripping process of the adsorbed hydrogen on the catalyst (in μC), and Q S is charge density for the stripping of hydrogen on a reference Pt electrode (220 μC cm−2) [30]. The Q H can be calculated by the following formula:
Q
H
=
1
v
∫
I d V
, where ν is the scan rate (in V s−1), I is current of hydrogen stripping (in μΑ), and V is the potential (in V). In order to calculate EAS values, the following formula is usually used for calculation:
E A S =
A
m
. In the above-mentioned formula, m is obtained by the TG data (Fig. 6A) and modified amounts of samples on electrode. By calculating, the EAS values of CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-1, CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-2, CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3, CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-4 and CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-5 are 6.23 m2 g−1, 5.27 m2 g−1, 10.12 m2 g−1, 4.92 m2 g−1 and 6.63 m2 g−1, respectively. Indubitably, the above data prove that EAS of CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3 is the largest, compared with other fabricated samples. As well known, high EAS of CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3 will lead to excellent electrocatalytic performance.

The comparison investigation of electrochemical response of 8-OH-dG at different electrodes were demonstrated. Fig. 7 A shows the cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-1/GCE, CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-2/GCE, CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE, CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-4/GCE, CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-5/GCE, NGR/GCE, CQDs@PtNCs/GCE, CQDs@PtNCs-NGR/GCE and bare GCE electrodes in 0.1 M pH 7.4 PBS, when 32.0 μM 8-OH-dG is set to play the role of oxidation probe. It is clearly see that 8-OH-dG on bare GCE (curve i) just has a small and poor anodic peak that appears at E
pa of 0.40 V, the oxidation peak current is just 1.04 μA. Compared to bare GCE, however, the E
pa at CQD@PtNCs/GCE (curve g) is much more positive shifted. The oxidation peak current on CQD@PtNCs/GCE (curve g) is 1.30 times as high as that on bare GCE. Interestingly, the E
pa at NGR/GCE (curve f) is much more negative shifted compared to bare GCE. Compared to CQD@PtNCs/GCE, the oxidation peak current on NGR/GCE is just slightly increase. The potential of CQDs@PDA@Pt NCs-NGR-2/GCE (curve b) shifted to 0.37 V, the oxidation peak current is obviously increased to 2.61 μA compared to 1.43 μA on NGR/GCE. Moreover, the peak current at CQDs@PDA@Pt NCs-NGR-1/GCE (curve a) enhanced about 1.39 times than that of CQDs@PDA@Pt NCs-NGR-2/GCE. It is noticeable that the peak current at CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-4/GCE (curved) is nearly unchanged compared to CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-1/GCE. In addition, the peak current at CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-5/GCE (curve e) enhanced about 1.41 times than that of CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-4/GCE. Compared to the peak current of CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-5/GCE (5.12 μA), the peak current of CQDs@PtNCs-NGR/GCE (curve h) is only increased to 5.55 μA. The most interesting thing is that the oxidation peak current is intensively increased to 8.43 μA at CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE (curve c) with attractive peak shape compared to CQDs@PtNCs-NGR/GCE, and the peak current of the former is just approximately 1.52 times than that of the latter. Especially, CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE gives rise to the largest oxidation peak current in all of the samples and 8.11 times as high as that of bare GCE. The superior electrochemical performance of CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE for 8-OH-dG could be ascribed to the smaller particle size of CQDs@PDA@PtNCs which leads to higher electroactive surface area, thereby achieving higher electrocatalytic performance.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) technology can be used to further identify the interfacial properties of the electrode surface (Fig. 7B). The experiment was adopted by employing the solution of 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3-/4- and 0.1 M KCl. The EIS consists of a semicircular part and a linear part. The semicircular part at higher frequencies corresponds to the electron transfer limited process, and the diameter is equivalent to the electron transfer resistance (R
ct ) [31,32]. Randles equivalence circuit model (insert in Fig. 7B) is used to fitting impedance data, where R s is Warburg impedence, C dl is double layer capacitance, W is the diffusion of ions from electrolyte and electrode interface and R ct is electron transfer resistance [33]. The R
ct of bare GCE is 172 Ω. In addition, the semicircle was remarkably changed to a straight line of CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-(1–5)/GCE and NGR/GCE, displaying that the above-mentioned electrodes have a very small impedance and powerful electrical conductivity. The conductivity of CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-(1–5)/GCE is significantly higher than that of bare GCE, indicating that CQDs@PDA@Pt NCs-NGR-(1–5) nanomaterials can form good electron pathways between the electrode and electrolyte and effectively promote the conductivity of the modified electrode.

CVs of CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE at different pH values from 3.0 to 10.0 were recorded for investigating the effect of pH on the oxidation peak of 8-OH-dG, and the results are shown in Fig. 8 A. As can be seen, the oxidation peak currents of 8-OH-dG increased gradually with increasing the pH until it achieved pH 7.0. Moreover, peak potential shifted negatively with the increasing of pH ranged from 3.0 to 10.0, suggesting that protons were participated in the electrode reaction. Additionally, we can clearly see that the peak current of 8-OH-dG significantly increased below pH 7.0 and then decreased with increased pH (Fig. 8B). Consequently, pH 7.0 was selected for the further experiments.

The number of transferred electrons of 8-OH-dG oxidation was studied using chronocoulometry (CC, Fig. 8C). It was calculated from the charge produced during the electrolysis of 8-OH-dG and using Faraday's law. The Faraday reaction is as follows:
n =
M Q
m F
Here M is the atomic weight of 8-OH-dG (283.24 g mol−1), F is the Faraday constant (96485 C mol−1), m is the mass of the fully electrolytic 8-OH-dG (1.39 × 10−8 g), Q is the charge after fully electrolysis of 8-OH-dG (8.65 μC). The number of transferred electrons of 8-OH-dG is calculated to be 1.83, which is approximately equal to 2.0. So the number of transferred electrons of 8-OH-dG oxidation is 2.0. In addition, as shown in Fig. 8B, linear relationship between peak potential of 8-OH-dG oxidation and pH values can be obtained:
E
(
V
)
= 0.830 + 0.062
p H
. The value of the slope equaled to 0.062 V pH−1, which is pretty close to the Nernstian theoretical value of 0.059 V pH−1. On the basis of Nernst equation: −0.062 m/n = −0.059, where n is defined as the number of transfer electron and m is regarded as the proton number. According to Faraday's law, the number of transferred electrons is 2.0. By calculating, the proton number of 8-OH-dG oxidation is 1.9, which is approximately equal to 2.0. Through the above calculation, the numbers of protons and electrons were equal (2.0) in the electrochemical process of 8-OH-dG oxidation. Scheme 2 shows the reaction mechanism of 8-OH-dG oxidation.

Moreover, the effect of scan rates on the electrochemical oxidation of 32.0 μM 8-OH-dG on CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE in 0.1 M pH 7.0 PBS was investigated. The results were showed in Fig. 9 A. One can see that the anodic peak currents increased linearly with the scan rate (Fig. 9A) and are proportional to the scan rate (5∼500 mV s−1) (Fig. 9B). The linear regression equation is:
I
(
μ A
)
= 0.392 + 0.067 V
(
v / s
)
, indicating that the oxidation of 8-OH-dG at CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE is an adsorption-controlled process.

As shown in Fig. 9C, the peak potential is linear with the logarithm of scan rate (5∼500 mV s−1) and the linear relationship can be expressed as:
E
p a
(
V
)
= 0.428 + 0.028
l o g
v
( mV / S )
According to
E
p a
= c o n s t a n t + 2.303 R T
L o g
v
( 1 − α )
n F
, wherein α states the transfer coefficient that characterizes the effect of electrochemical potential on activation energy of an electrochemical reaction, n is the number of transfer electron, ν is the scan rate. Thus, the RT/(1-α)nF is easily obtained from the slope of above-mentioned line. Generally, α is supposed to be 0.5 for an irreversible electrode process, so n is calculated to be 2. Consequently, the electrochemical mechanism of 8-OH-dG on CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE is a two-electron two proton process, it is consistent with the result obtained by chronocoulometry.

Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) is a highly sensitive and selective method for quantitative analysis. Accordingly, the electrochemical determination of 8-OH-dG was examined using DPV at the CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE under the stated optimal conditions. Fig. 10 A shows the DPVs of 8-OH-dG at CQDs@PDA@Pt NCs-NGR-3/GCE in 0.1 M pH 7.0 PBS. Inset of Fig. 10A shows the DPVs of 8-OH-dG at CQD@PDA@Pt NCs-NGR-3/GCE with low concentrations. All the DPV curves were processed through baseline correction, which could make the baseline smooth and more favorable for observing the current change of the analyte. Moreover, baseline correction can deduct background current generated by analysis conditions and system performance. We can clearly see that the DPV curve of blank solution (0.1 M PBS) without 8-OH-dG is almost a straight line and the current is almost zero (curve a in inset of Fig. 10A), and when 8-OH-dG is added, the peak arise and peak current increases with the increase of 8-OH-dG concentration. As shown in Fig. 10B, it can be obviously found that the peak current is linearly related to the 8-OH-dG concentration over two concentration range, the linear range are 0.013–16.68 μM and 16.68–109.78 μM, respectively. The linear regression equations are as follows:
I
(
μ A
)
= 0.718 + 0.559
∁
8 − O H − d G
(
μ M
)
( R = 0.996 )
I
(
μ A
)
= 4.503 + 0.296
∁
8 − O H − d G
(
μ M
)
( R = 0.999 )

The high sensitivity of 7.912 μA μM−1cm−2 and 4.190 μA μM−1cm−2 are obtained, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) is the lowest concentration of an analyte in a sample that can be determined with acceptable precision and accuracy under the stated conditions of test [34]. Generally speaking, LOD is calculated based on the signal to noise ratio (3 times noise level) and calculated by the following formula [34]:
D =
3 σ
k

Where σ stands for the standard deviation of the blank (μA), k is the slope of a linear equation (μA μM−1) [35–38]. In this work, the noise is obtained from the standard deviation of the DPV signal of the blank experiment under the same conditions (n = 10). Specifically, by calculating the standard deviation of ten blank current values under the potential of 0.38 V, σ value of 8.38 × 10−5 μA can be obtained. Since the electrochemical quantitative detection of 8-OH-dG has two linear range and two linear equations, it also has two slopes (k 1 = 0.559 μA μM−1, k 2 = 0.296 μA μM−1). Therefore, there are two low detection limits (S/N = 3), which are estimated to be 0.45 nM and 0.85 nM, respectively. Compared with other previously reported works (Table 1 ) [39–44], CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE shows higher electrocatalytic performance for 8-OH-dG detection with higher sensitivity, wider linear range and lower detection limit.

Deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA), as a vital biomolecule, can be attacked by reactive oxygen species (ROS) to generate 8-OH-dG, which is widely considered as a biomarker of oxidative DNA damage [45]. To further testify the product of damaged DNA is 8-OH-dG, DPV was adopted for researching. As shown in Fig. 11 A, no peak current was observed in (a) 0.1 M PBS (pH 5.0) (blank), (b) Fenton reagents (800 μL, in blank) and (c) undamaged DNA. On the contrary, when DNA was damaged by ·OH produced from Fenton reaction, a DPV oxidation peak appeared at 0.55 V (curve d). To test and verify it was attributable to the oxidation of 8-OH-dG originated from the damaged DNA, 5 μL 8-OH-dG (1.77 mM) was added into the above damaged DNA solution (curve e). Clearly, the oxidation peak current is exactly increased to 0.032 μA at 0.56 V (Fig. 11A curve e), illustrating 8-OH-dG is the oxidation product of DNA damage.

G plays a key role in biological genetics, but as one of the four nitrogenous bases in DNA, it is most easily oxidized. G plays an important role in cell aging process and tumor cell reproduction. Therefore, the assessment of concentration levels of G is considered to be a vital parameter in physiology and clinical pathology [46]. In order to better understanding of damage processes in the DNA structure and its biological consequences, we also studied damaged G with a similar method. As shown in Fig. 11B, similar to Fig. 11A, one can see that there are almost straight lines on CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE by DPVs in 0.1 M PBS (pH 5.0), Fenton reagents (800 μL, blank) and the undamaged G, respectively. Nevertheless, after adding damaged G in PBS, the oxidation peak is occurred at 0.53 V (Fig. 11B curve d). As we expected, after adding of 15 μL 8-OH-dG (0.8 mM) in the damaged G solution, the oxidation peak current increases to 0.728 μA (Fig. 11B curve e) compared with damaged G (0.618 μA). Therefore, combined with the result from Fig. 11A, we can get the conclusion that 8-OH-dG is identified as the oxidation product of damaged G in DNA.

8-OH-dG is an oxidized product of the deoxyguanosine residues in DNA, which is engendered by the attack of reactive oxygen species (like hydroxyl radical) at the C-8 position of G. In addition, the level of 8-OH-dG in urine is directly related to the degree of oxidative damage of DNA [47]. In urine sample, UA is the primary end product of purine metabolism [48], coexists in excess of 8-OH-dG and the structure is extremely resembled to 8-OH-dG. Usually, the normal level of UA in serum is in the range of 0.13–0.46 mM (130–460 μM) [49]. For a better understanding of the interference of UA with the electrochemical determination of 8-OH-dG, CV of CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE for UA detection in the presence of 19.6 μM 8-OH-dG (Fig. 12 A) was researched. The peak currents of UA increase with the increased concentrations (5.2–644.8 μM) while the peak current of 8-OH-dG almost unchanged, revealing that the presence of 8-OH-dG doesn't affect the determination of UA. In addition, as shown in Fig. 12B, it can be clearly seen that the anodic peak currents of 8-OH-dG increase with the increased concentrations from 5.3 μM to 102.4 μM, while the peak currents of 41.7 μM UA change slightly, which exhibits 8-OH-dG can also be accurately quantified determination in the presence of UA. Hence, high selectivity detection of UA and 8-OH-dG at the CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE can be achieved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The repeatability, reproducibility and stability research
ce:section.ce:para: The repeatability, reproducibility and stability of the electrocatalyst are significant issues in the application [50]. The repeatability of CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE for 8-OH-dG determination was evaluated by using the same electrode for 10 successive assays with five different concentrations (20, 40, 60, 80 and 100 μM) (n = 10). By calculating, the standard deviation (SD) is 7.05 × 10−3 μA μM−1, the average value is 0.32 μA μM−1 and the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the slopes of the calibration curves obtained for each of the linear sections is 2.26%. Furthermore, we analyzed the reproducibility of CQDs@PDA@Pt NCs-NGR-3/GCE for 8-OH-dG determination by using 10 differently electrodes at five diverse concentrations of 8-OH-dG (20, 40, 60, 80 and 100 μM) (n = 10). Through calculating, the SD is 8.03 × 10−3 μA μM−1, the average value is 0.31 μA μM−1 and the RSD of the slopes of the calibration curves obtained for each of the linear sections is 2.60%. These results demonstrate that the prepared CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE has excellent repeatability and reproducibility for 8-OH-dG detection.

The stability of the CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE for 8-OH-dG determination was researched. The CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE was stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C for one week and measured 29.3 μM 8-OH-dG for 10 times every 1 day. During the 7 days, the response current decreased by 1.2%, 2.0%, 2.2%, 5.8%, 6.2%, 7.5% and 8.4% of its initial response, respectively. In other words, the CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE was stored at 4 °C for one week, during that time, 70 successive assays were done, and 91.6% of the initial current response was retained, this indicated good stability of CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE for 8-OH-dG detection.

To validate the biological applicability of the developed CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3, it was used for the analysis of 8-OH-dG content in complex matrix such as urine by using the standard addition method. The urine sample of a healthy volunteer was collected for performing the biological assay, the urine samples were centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 20 min to remove deposition. The collected urine supernatants were diluted by using 0.10 M pH 7.0 PBS at the dilution proportion of 1: 10 and 1: 1, respectively. The experiment results were shown in Table 2 . The spiked recovery values of 99.96%, 101.50%, 103.70% were obtained with one-fold-diluted samples (n = 3) for 8-OH-dG. Meanwhile, the spiked recoveries were 98.49%, 93.95%, 97.99% with ten-fold-diluted samples (n = 3). The satisfactory recoveries for 8-OH-dG suggest that the proposed CQDs@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3/GCE may provide a potential application for the determination of 8-OH-dG in real samples.

In summary, for the first time, carbon quantum dots encapsulated in super small platinum nanocrystals core-shell architecture/nitrogen doped graphene hybrid nanocomposite (CQD@PDA@PtNCs/NGR) was prepared. No capping reagent, stabilizer and surfactant, which were usually harmful for catalytic activities, were used in the experiments, and high dispersed super small platinum nanocrystals with average diameter of 2.25 nm was prepared. This work demonstrates that CQD@PDA@PtNCs-NGR-3 improves the electrocatalytic performance, sensitivity and selectivity of the electrocatalyst for the determination of 8-OH-dG generated from damaged DNA. Our work sets up a new avenue for the development of an ideal candidate for electrochemical research the mechanism of DNA damage, it is of great significance in biomedical area.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21505031), the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (No. B2018201214, No. B2016201018), colleges and universities science technology research project of Hebei Province (No. Z2015096).
